methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $60M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: planqc

data.narrative:
Nu Quantum, a Series A quantum computing company founded by Carmen Palacios-Berraquero, is evaluated within a sector universe of 11 peers: 3 public (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private entities including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion. The company has raised $60M to date.

Stage-progression methodology was the sole applicable triangulation approach. Using total capital raised ($60M) as a proxy for current valuation and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement yields an implied valuation of $120M. This reflects the conventional markup investors apply when companies progress toward Series B. Peer-median analysis was not applicable: while 7 same-sector peers exist, filtering for same-stage or adjacent-stage companies with similar valuation tiers yielded fewer than 3 datapoints, making a statistically defensible median calculation impossible. Relying on all-sector medians would introduce unacceptable noise given quantum computing's heterogeneity in technology approaches and commercialization timelines.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $102M to $138M, centered on $120M. This relatively narrow band reflects reliance on a single method rather than true triangulation. Key uncertainties include the appropriate step-up multiplier for quantum computing specifically—traditional 2.0x assumptions may not apply if investor behavior in this sector diverges from broader venture norms—and the absence of direct peer comparables at identical stage and scale, limiting cross-validation of the stage-progression output.
